title: Bangers and mash
subtitle: Sausages, mash, slow-cooked onion gravy.
excerpt: Good pork sausages browned in a pan, served on a deep mound of buttered mashed potato under a slow-caramelised onion gravy. Pub-classic comfort food in forty-five minutes. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), on browning sausages slowly to render the fat without splitting the skin. Onion-gravy method draws on Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), public domain, who specifies the long, dry caramelisation of onion before any flour or stock is added.

recipe.freezeNotes: Sausages and mash don't keep their texture from the freezer. The onion gravy alone freezes for three months — make a double batch.
recipe.batchNotes: Onion gravy doubles or triples easily and freezes flat. Mash for four is the upper limit of comfortable one-batch cooking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Caramelise the onions up to two days ahead; warm with stock on the day. Mash is best made fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: Onions cooked low and slow until their sugars brown to a deep mahogany. Twenty to thirty minutes; never rush with high heat, which scorches the surface and leaves the inside raw.

content.text: Eight good pork sausages browned slowly in a heavy pan, plated on a mound of buttery mashed potato, all flooded with onion gravy that has cooked its onions down to dark sweet ribbons before any flour, stock, or wine joined in. Forty-five minutes from kitchen to table. Comfort food without fuss.

The whole dish hangs on three things: good sausages, dry mash, and onions cooked properly. Cheap sausages are mostly rusk and water; they shed liquid into the pan and never brown. Wet mash slumps; the gravy slides off it. Onions taken to a sticky deep-brown sweetness make the gravy. Get those three right and the rest is assembly.

items.prepNote: good butcher's pork sausages, ~80 g each

content.text: Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter with {{rapeseed-oil}} of rapeseed oil in a heavy frying pan over a low hob. Tip in {{onion}} of sliced onions with a pinch of salt and the {{thyme}} of thyme leaves. Stir to coat. Cook gently for 20 to 25 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until the onions collapse into sticky dark
. Don't try to speed this with high heat — the onions scorch on the outside and stay raw on the inside.

While the onions cook, cover {{potato}} of quartered potatoes with cold salted water. Bring to the boil. Simmer 18 minutes until a knife slips in cleanly. Drain. Return to the dry hot pan for one minute to steam off the surface water; this is the difference between cloud mash and gluey mash.

Heat a second frying pan over medium heat with a teaspoon of oil. Lay {{sausages-pork}} sausages in the pan in a single layer. Cook for 12 to 14 minutes, turning every three or four minutes, until evenly bronzed all over. The skins should be dark and the centres firm to the touch. If they brown too fast and the inside hasn't caught up, drop the heat.
Mash the steamed-dry potatoes. Beat in {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in pieces, then {{whole-milk}} of warmed milk. Season generously with salt and pepper. Keep warm with a lid on a low hob.

When the onions are dark and sticky, sprinkle in {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for one minute to cook out the raw taste. Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine if using; let it bubble down for two minutes. Add {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire and {{english-mustard}} of English mustard. Pour in {{stock-beef}} of hot stock in three stages, stirring smooth between each. Simmer for five minutes until the gravy coats the back of a spoon.
Pile mash in the centre of four warm plates. Sit two sausages against the mash. Spoon the onion gravy over both, generously enough that it pools at the bottom of the plate.

items.cause: Cooked too hot or pricked with a fork.
items.fix: Medium heat, no pricking. Turn every three minutes.

items.symptom: Sausages weep liquid and won't brown
items.cause: Cheap sausages high in water and rusk.
items.fix: Buy butcher's pork sausages with 80%+ pork. Drain any water that pools and finish browning.

items.cause: Heat too high, not enough fat.
items.fix: Lowest hob setting. Stir often. Add a splash of water if they stick before they soften.

items.cause: Flour added straight to stock, or not cooked out.
items.fix: Stir the flour into the dry onions and cook for a full minute before any liquid joins.
Maris Piper, King Edward, or Russet. Mash by hand. Never blend or food-process.

content.text: Cumberland sausages (long coiled herbed pork) are the classic. Lincolnshire sausages bring sage. Toulouse swap in a Frenchier register with garlic and wine in the meat. Vegetarian: use a good plant-based banger and switch the beef stock for vegetable; the onion gravy with a teaspoon of marmite stirred in carries the depth that beef stock would. Peas, buttered cabbage, or wilted spinach on the side.

Bangers and mash sits at the heart of British pub food and the weeknight kitchen alike. The 'banger' nickname comes from wartime sausages with high water content that exploded in the pan; the name stuck even after the meat ratio recovered. A dish that asks little of the cook and gives much back: half an hour at the hob, food that comforts on a wet Tuesday in November.
